card.intuition: Visit all nodes at depth d before any node at depth d+1 — use a queue so the first nodes in are the first processed, producing level-by-level output.
card.analogy: Photographing a family reunion by generation: grandparents first, then parents, then children — you walk the entire row before moving to the next.
card.space_complexity: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree — O(n) worst case for a complete tree's last level
when_to_use: Level-order output, zigzag traversal, average of levels
Minimum depth of a binary tree — BFS finds the first leaf faster than DFS
Right-side view — keep only the last node at each level
Connect nodes at the same level (populating next pointers)
gotchas: Use a double-ended queue (deque) not a list for O(1) popleft — list.pop(0) is O(n)
To separate levels, either snapshot queue length at the start of each level, or use a sentinel None between levels
A None child in a complete binary tree representation is NOT a node — don't enqueue it

exercise.prompt: Return a list of lists — each inner list contains the values at one level of the tree.
hints: Enqueue the left child (node.left) if it exists
Enqueue the right child (node.right) if it exists — children go to the back of the queue for the next level
exercise.test_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self,v,l=None,r=None): self.val=v;self.left=l;self.right=r

root=TreeNode(3,TreeNode(9),TreeNode(20,TreeNode(15),TreeNode(7)))
assert level_order(root) == [[3],[9,20],[15,7]], 'Standard tree'
assert level_order(TreeNode(1)) == [[1]], 'Single node'
assert level_order(None) == [], 'Empty tree'
root2=TreeNode(1,TreeNode(2,TreeNode(4),TreeNode(5)),TreeNode(3))
assert level_order(root2) == [[1],[2,3],[4,5]], 'Asymmetric tree'
print('PASS')
